purpose: Quantifies the nonlinear, convex relationship between interest rate changes and equity fundamental value through the Economic Value Added (EVA) identity, demonstrating that current compressed spreads make equities ~1.5-2.0x more rate-sensitive than in any prior positive correlation episode.
components: EVA identity: EVA = Invested Capital × (ROIC - WACC)
At historical 7pp spread: 100bp WACC increase → ~14% EVA destruction
At current ~4pp spread: 100bp WACC increase → ~25% EVA destruction (1.8x amplification)
Impact bifurcation: mega-cap tech (net cash, 1-5% interest/EBIT) vs. S&P 493 (18%+ interest/EBIT)
current_assessment: This is a mathematical identity, not an empirical claim — labeled 'indisputable' by pair_1. Rate increases now damage equity earnings through the fundamental channel (not just multiple compression) approximately 1.5-2.0x more powerfully than any prior positive correlation episode. The structural implication that consensus models systematically underestimate equity vulnerability survives all debate.
limitations: Precise current spread estimate has ±50bp uncertainty
Does not account for sector-level variation in ROIC-WACC dynamics
Aggregate framework may mask offsetting movements within the index
Does not specify the timing of fundamental earnings impact vs. market price reaction
Corporate debt maturity wall interaction amplifies the effect but timing is uncertain
